# **Architectural Design of a Monolithic Sorting Network for Tournament Progression Visualization within the SportsEngine AES Ecosystem**

The organizational complexity inherent in multi-day volleyball tournaments necessitated the development of a unified, high-integrity data visualization framework. Currently, the Advanced Event Systems (AES), a subsidiary of SportsEngine, serves as the primary repository for scheduling and results data in the volleyball community.1 However, the dissemination of critical information within this platform is characterized by high levels of fragmentation. Stakeholders—including coaches, athletes, and spectators—are frequently required to traverse a labyrinth of disconnected web pages, ranging from divisional overviews to specific pool sheets and bracket diagrams.3 This architectural proposal delineates the creation of a monolithic graph-based sorting network that consolidates these disparate data points into a single, interactive interface hosted via GitHub Pages and automated through GitHub Actions. By modeling the tournament as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), the system provides real-time path prediction, versioned state management, and comprehensive traceability, ensuring that every user can visualize their team’s trajectory from the initial seed to the final classification.5

## **Theoretical Foundation: The Tournament as a Sorting Network**

A fundamental conceptual shift is required to move from static table-based schedules to a dynamic monolithic graph. A volleyball tournament, at its mathematical core, is a sorting network designed to rank ![][image1] teams through a series of pairwise comparisons (matches). In a sorting network, teams are fed into the system from the left (the initial sort) and emerge on the right in their final ranked positions (the final sort).7 Each match acts as a comparator node where the winning team follows one path and the losing team follows another.

### **Mathematical Modeling of Tournament Progression**

The tournament is modeled as a Directed Acyclic Graph ![][image2], where ![][image3] represents the set of nodes (match-ups, pool placements, or bracket positions) and ![][image4] represents the set of directed edges (team movement paths). Each team ![][image5] is an entity flowing through the network. The reachability of a team can be expressed as a function of the set of all possible paths ![][image6] from the team's current node ![][image7] to any terminal node ![][image8] in the "Sink" layer (the final classification).

The complexity of the sorting network is defined by the number of rounds ![][image9] and the connectivity of the comparators. For a standard double-elimination bracket, the depth of the network is approximately:

![][image10]  
where ![][image1] is the number of teams and ![][image11] represents additional crossover or challenge rounds typically found in AES-scheduled tournaments.1 The proposed architecture utilizes these mathematical properties to calculate team trajectories in real-time, pruning invalid paths as match results are ingested.5

### **Comparative State Analysis**

The sorting network must maintain a deterministic state across various tournament phases. The following table illustrates the conceptual mapping of tournament components to graph properties within the proposed monolithic architecture.

| Tournament Concept | Graph Component | Data Attribute Mapping |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Initial Seeding | Source Nodes (Left) | Pre-tournament Power Ranking 11 |
| Match Match-up | Comparator Node | Location, Court Number, Time 13 |
| Pool Play | Sub-graph / Cluster | Round-robin connectivity 3 |
| Classification Result | Sink Nodes (Right) | Final Divisional Standing 15 |
| Follow-on Instruction | Directed Edge | "1st-R1-P1 to Gold Match 1" logic 14 |
| Match Result | Node State Change | Color-coding and Edge pruning 18 |

## **Data Ingestion and Scraping Architecture**

The integrity of the visualization depends entirely on the robust extraction of data from the SportsEngine AES web structure. Since AES relies heavily on client-side rendering for its results pages, a traditional static HTML scraper is insufficient.20 The ingestion engine must be capable of interpreting dynamic content and navigating the hierarchical relationship between events, divisions, pools, and brackets.9

### **Scraper Design for Dynamic Environments**

The ingestion module is implemented using a headless browser automation framework such as Playwright or Selenium, which ensures that all JavaScript-rendered components—such as the "Pool Sheets" and "Bracket Views"—are fully loaded before data extraction.20 The scraper follows a recursive discovery pattern: it begins with the unique Event ID (e.g., PTAwMDAwNDEwNjg90) and traverses the divisional landing pages to identify all relevant sub-components.9

A critical challenge in AES scraping is the handling of "JavaScript Required" blocks and asynchronous loading.21 The architecture addresses this by implementing a retry mechanism with exponential backoff and validating the presence of key DOM elements, such as the Team Name or Pool Sheet headers, before attempting to parse the page.9 This approach ensures that the scraper can reliably capture match results, set scores, and follow-on instructions even under heavy server load or during site-wide updates.

### **The Logic of Follow-on Parsing**

One of the most complex requirements is the ability to determine where a team will play next based on their performance. AES pool sheets contain textual cues for this progression, often encoded in strings like "1st-R1-B E" or "R2-BD1A".14 The ingestion engine includes a specialized parser that uses regular expressions to decode these follow-on instructions and map them to the corresponding nodes in the global graph.

This follow-on logic is essential for the "possible positions" functionality. By parsing strings such as "1st Place moves to Gold, 2nd Place to Silver," the system constructs the full set of future edges for every team.15 This data is then serialized into a JSON manifest that serves as the source of truth for the visualization engine.23

| Data Source | Targeted Information | Extraction Method |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Division Standings | Initial seeds, current rankings, set/point ratios | Table parsing with BeautifulSoup 25 |
| Pool Sheets | Match times, opponents, follow-on logic codes | Recursive sub-page crawling 14 |
| Bracket Views | Knockout structure, winner/loser path assignments | SVG/HTML path mapping 1 |
| Team Pages | Individual schedules, work team duties, links | Direct URL targeting 4 |

## **Transformation Layer and State Management**

Once the raw data is extracted, it must be transformed into a structured format that supports the interactive features of the monolithic graph. This transformation layer is responsible for maintaining the graph’s integrity, managing versioned updates, and implementing the path pruning logic that follows each completed match.

### **Graph Construction and Versioning**

The transformation script converts the ingested JSON data into a Cytoscape-compatible format, where nodes are enriched with metadata such as court location, match time, and original AES links.18 To fulfill the requirement for versioning, each run of the GitHub Action captures the current state and commits the generated JSON to the Git history. The front-end utilizes these commit hashes to allow users to "flip back and forth" between different time-states, providing a historical view of the tournament’s progression.30

This versioning mechanism is not merely for audit purposes; it allows the system to calculate the "velocity" of a tournament—how quickly pools are finishing and whether subsequent rounds are likely to start early or late. By comparing the Updated At timestamps in the manifest across versions, the system can provide predictive alerts to teams about potential schedule shifts.3

### **Pruning and Path Validity Logic**

The core of the interactive experience is the ability to filter paths based on known results. When a match node transitions from a "Pending" state to a "Finished" state, the transformation layer executes a reachability update.

![][image12]  
If Team A defeats Team B in a quarterfinal match, all edges originating from the "Winner" out-port of that match node are assigned to Team A, while edges originating from the "Loser" out-port are assigned to Team B.5 Paths that were previously "possible" for Team B (such as reaching the championship) are now pruned from the graph. The visualization engine then updates the opacity or visibility of these edges, ensuring that only the "valid paths" remain prominent.10

## **Interactive Visualization Interface**

The user-facing component of the project is a single-page application (SPA) that renders the monolithic graph using Cytoscape.js. This library was selected over D3.js due to its superior support for directed acyclic graph layouts and its built-in graph theory algorithms, which are necessary for the path-highlighting features.18

### **Monolithic Graph Layout and Styling**

The graph employs the Dagre layout algorithm, which is optimized for hierarchical structures. It automatically positions the initial seeding on the far left and the final standings on the far right, ensuring a logical flow of time and progression across the screen.34 Nodes are color-coded and shaped according to the user's requirements to maximize information density without causing visual fatigue.

* **Node Shapes:** The shape of each node is dynamically determined by the location attribute. For example, matches held in the "Main Arena" might be represented by circles, while those in "Satellite Courts" are represented by rectangles.19 This allows users to instantly recognize venue transitions.  
* **Color Encoding:** Node background colors reflect the status of the match—gray for scheduled, blue for in-progress, and green/red for the selected team’s win/loss status.19  
* **Court Numbers:** Each node includes a small, high-contrast label representing the specific court number at the location, ensuring that participants can quickly find their assignment.13  
* **Tooltips and Popups:** Mousing over a node triggers a floating popup (implemented via Tippy.js) that displays the final set scores, the work team assigned to the match, and a direct link back to the original AES pool sheet.15

### **Team-Specific Trajectory Analysis**

When a user selects a specific team from a dropdown menu or by clicking a source node, the interface activates a "Trajectory Mode." This mode utilizes the BFS (Breadth-First Search) algorithm to highlight all possible downstream nodes and edges that the selected team can reach.10

In "Initial State" (before play begins), a high-seeded team will see a wide fan of potential paths leading to various bracket positions. As games finish, the highlighted paths narrow. This feature directly solves the user's problem of "traversing links across multiple pages" by providing a single, visual answer to the question of team destiny.4 The interface also supports "Backward Traversal," allowing a user at a terminal node to see exactly which sequence of wins and losses led a team to that specific ranking.10

## **Deployment, Automation, and Export Functionality**

The architecture leverages the GitHub ecosystem for all stages of the lifecycle, from development to live hosting. This ensures that the project remains open-source, reproducible, and requires zero maintenance costs for traditional server infrastructure.44

### **GitHub Actions and Pages Integration**

The system is automated through a YAML-based workflow in GitHub Actions. This workflow is triggered by a cron schedule (e.g., every 10 minutes during active tournament hours) or manually via workflow\_dispatch.46 The action performs a clean checkout of the repository, executes the Python ingestion script, and uses a deployment action to push the updated static site to the gh-pages branch.48

This serverless approach is highly resilient. Because the results are pre-computed and stored as static JSON manifests, the website can handle high traffic spikes common during tournament finals without performance degradation.49 Furthermore, the use of actions/upload-pages-artifact ensures that the deployment process is secure and integrated into GitHub's official hosting pipeline.49

### **Export and Traceability Tools**

To facilitate sharing and offline use, the interface includes an "Export" module. Users can generate high-resolution PNG or SVG versions of the current graph view using the Cytoscape.js internal rendering engine.52 For professional-grade reporting, the system integrates jsPDF and svg2pdf.js to create multi-page PDF documents that preserve the vector-based clarity of the graph.54

Traceability is maintained by embedding the original AES URLs as metadata within each node. Every match result or team standing displayed in the monolithic graph includes a "Source" link that opens the corresponding SportsEngine page in a new tab.13 This ensures that the custom tool remains a valid extension of the official record, rather than a disconnected silo.

| Feature | Implementation Technology | Requirement Satisfied |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Versioning | Git Commit History 30 | Ability to flip back and forth through states |
| Automation | GitHub Actions 47 | Zero-manual-intervention updates |
| Hosting | GitHub Pages 45 | Static, resilient web hosting |
| Export | jsPDF / html2canvas 57 | PDF/PNG/SVG generation |
| Interactivity | Cytoscape.js Algorithms 10 | Team-specific path highlighting |

## **Adherence to Key Development Principles**

The project's architecture is strictly governed by the seven requested development principles, ensuring that the system is not only functional but also maintainable and logically sound.59

### **KISS, YAGNI, and Modularity**

The **KISS (Keep It Simple Stupid)** principle is applied to the data storage strategy. Rather than implementing a complex SQL database, the system uses flat JSON files. This simplifies the ingestion pipeline and eliminates the need for database migrations or a dedicated backend.24 The **YAGNI (You Ain't Gonna Need It)** principle guided the decision to exclude probabilistic match prediction; the system focuses purely on deterministic path visualization, which is the user’s primary need.

**Modularity** is achieved by decoupling the scraper from the graph generator. The scraper's only responsibility is to output a raw JSON dump of the AES data, while the transformation layer converts this dump into a graph structure.59 This separation allows the scraper to be updated if the AES website structure changes without affecting the core visualization logic.

### **SOLID and DRY in Ingestion Logic**

The **SOLID** principles are most evident in the scraper's class design. Each parser (e.g., PoolSheetParser, BracketParser, StandingsParser) follows the Single Responsibility Principle, and the system is Open/Closed—new tournament formats can be supported by adding new parser modules without modifying the existing ones.60 The **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)** principle is maintained through shared utility functions for decoding AES progression codes (e.g., the logic to map "R1" to "Round 1" is centralized).

### **TDD and POLA in the User Experience**

**Test-Driven Development (TDD)** is utilized to ensure the reliability of the ingestion engine. A suite of unit tests validates the parser against historical AES HTML samples, ensuring that edge cases—such as three-way ties or "forfeits"—are handled correctly before the code is deployed.20 Finally, the **Principle of Least Astonishment (POLA)** governs the UI design. By using standard colors (green for win, red for loss) and intuitive gestures (click to select, hover for info), the system ensures that parents and coaches can use the tool without needing a manual.18

## **Conclusion and Future Outlook**

The architectural design of the monolithic tournament sorting network represents a significant advancement in sports data visualization. By consolidating fragmented SportsEngine AES data into a single, interactive DAG, the project eliminates the cognitive burden of navigating multiple schedule pages and provides teams with a powerful tool for strategic planning. The reliance on GitHub’s automation and hosting infrastructure ensures long-term viability and zero operational costs. As volleyball tournaments continue to grow in scale and complexity, this monolithic graph approach offers a scalable, high-integrity solution for tracking the "Sorting Network" of team progression from start to finish. Future enhancements may include the integration of live stream overlays into the node tooltips, further bridging the gap between physical tournament play and its digital representation.62
